text: The camera is angled 30 degrees downward, providing a view of the surgical field as the surgeon prepares to close the posterior defect of the incisional hernia.
The surgical field reveals one larger gap and several smaller gaps in the abdominal wall, indicative of multiple incisional hernias.
The right posterior rectus sheath is visible as a white structure on the far side of the screen.
The surgeon uses the peritoneum and pseudosac to bridge the gap between the edges of the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc 180 suture to sew the posterior sheath to the peritoneum, closing the visible gaps between these structures.
The surgeon carefully places sutures near the peritoneum, ensuring the small bowel adjacent to the peritoneum is not injured during the procedure.
The surgeon sutures tissue, attempting to differentiate between fatty omentum, preperitoneal fat, neoperitoneum, and adhesions, which are visually similar.
The surgeon identifies the fat as a combination of peritoneum, omentum, and neoperitoneum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully repairs each visible defect to address concerns about interparietal hernia, ensuring thorough closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon searches for smaller defects located in the syphilis portion of the surgical space.
The surgeon identifies the white structure on the right as the patient's left posterior rectal sheath.
A gap in the peritoneum is visible medial to the previously identified structures.
The surgeon observes bowel located underneath the peritoneum, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon examines the hernia sac, searching for any material within it that needs to be reduced.
The video shows a large abdominal defect with multiple Swiss cheese-like openings and diastasis, indicating encephalitis in the patient.
The surgeon selects an OV lock, 180, 18-inch suture for the procedure.
The surgeon adjusts their approach to reach the dome more easily, allowing them to grasp the hernia pseudosac.
The surgeon takes a firm bite of the linea alba, incorporating both the anterior rectus sheath and the cut posterior rectus sheath veil.
The surgeon cuts the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, making the posterior rectus sheath more visible compared to cutting closer to the linea alba.
The surgeon observes that the defect in the linea alba comes together easily, allowing for complete closure without significant resistance.
The surgeon takes small bites of the hernia pseudosac using a suture, aiming to reduce the size of the seroma by progressively closing the defect.
The surgeon progressively tensions the suture, adjusting its tightness as it is applied to the tissue.
The surgeon pulls the suture until there is visible resistance, indicating tension in the suture line.
The surgeon adjusts the suture to provide extra length, preparing to continue suturing the linea alba and pseudosac.
The surgeon runs out of suture and goes back to apply additional tension to the existing suture line.
The surgeon performs a final pull on the suture, pulling towards themselves while using the left hand to brace the surrounding tissue, ensuring proper tension.
The surgeon adjusts the suture to prevent it from cutting into the ipsilateral linea alba, ensuring proper tension and alignment during closure.
The surgeon pulls on the suture with noticeable tension, indicating excessive force being applied during the suturing process.
The surgeon adjusts the position medially, causing the ipsilateral abdominal wall to shift towards the midline, aligning with the contralateral side.
The surgeon uses their left hand to brace the tissue while pulling the suture towards themselves to close the defect.
The surgeon moves towards the cephalad portion of the defect closure, encountering difficulty with needle efficiency due to the suturing angle.
The surgeon tightens the last section of the suture and prepares to perform an image inversion.
The video shows the instruments labeled two and four switching positions at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon holds a needle driver in the right hand, demonstrating an improved angle for suturing, allowing for more efficient and faster stitching.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to take substantial bites of the Linea Alba, incorporating both the anterior rectus sheath and the posterior rectus sheath during suturing.
The surgeon avoids taking bites in the center due to the narrow diastasis, focusing on suturing along the edges.
The surgeon progressively tensions the suture, ensuring it is not overly tight, while closing the defect.
Using image inversion, the surgeon quickly sutures the visible defect together, demonstrating efficient closure of the tissue gap.
The surgeon successfully brings the edges of the defect together, demonstrating the closure of the gap.
The surgeon closes the defect and runs the suture line by grasping the veil of the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon runs the suture line back along the closed defect, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a few additional suture bites to lock the stitch as the suture length runs out.
The surgeon prepares to place a surgical mesh, likely positioning it within the operative field.
The surgeon positions the mesh to cover the entire floor of the surgical area, ensuring complete coverage rather than placing it on the roof.
The surgeon measures the width and length of the defect, preparing to place a mesh over the surgical area.
The surgeon assesses the space, taking multiple measurements of the width along specified sections of the length to determine its shape.
The surgeon observes a simple single space, preparing to place a mesh over the floor of the defect.
The surgeon displays a mesh approximately thirty-two centimeters long, preparing to cover the floor of the defect.
The surgeon measures the width of the defect, ensuring accurate dimensions for the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon places a 30 by 45 cm sheet of mesh into the surgical space through an 8 mm port, demonstrating that upsizing the port is unnecessary.
The mesh is positioned under the diaphragm, ensuring a good overlap above the diaphragm for secure placement.
The surgeon observes the mesh placement, noting its redundancy in the current case.
The lightweight mesh is visible folding and doubling on itself within the surgical field.
The surgeon trims the oversized mesh within the abdominal cavity to ensure proper fit and placement.
The surgeon uses scissors to trim the lightweight mesh within the abdominal cavity, demonstrating the ease of cutting due to the mesh's lightweight nature.
The surgeon decides not to trim the lightweight mesh, allowing it to fold and double on itself within the abdominal cavity.
